parentQuestion: The parasympathetic division stimulates ciliary muscles of the eye, making the lens bulge for close vision.

scaffolds.q: Your eye can focus on both near and far objects - this is called accommodation.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Accommodation is the ability to change lens shape to focus at different distances. It's like autofocus on a camera.

scaffolds.q: The lens is held in place by suspensory ligaments attached to the ciliary muscle.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The zonule fibers (suspensory ligaments) connect the lens to the ciliary body. When the ciliary muscle contracts, it changes the tension.

scaffolds.q: The lens is naturally elastic - it WANTS to be round/spherical.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Like a rubber ball, the lens has natural elasticity pushing it toward a rounder shape.

scaffolds.q: When suspensory ligaments are TAUT, they pull the lens flat for distance vision.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! At rest, the ligaments are stretched and pulling - flattening the lens for focusing on distant objects.

scaffolds.q: When the ciliary muscle CONTRACTS, it releases tension on the ligaments.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The ring-shaped ciliary muscle tightens inward, bringing attachment points closer together, releasing tension on the zonule fibers.

scaffolds.q: When ligament tension releases, the elastic lens bulges into a rounder shape.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! With no pulling force, the lens springs into its natural round shape - more refractive power for near focus.

scaffolds.q: Ciliary muscle contraction is controlled by parasympathetic fibers from CN III.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The oculomotor nerve (CN III) carries parasympathetic fibers to the ciliary ganglion, then to the ciliary muscle.

scaffolds.q: Parasympathetic stimulates ciliary muscle → lens bulges → near vision focus.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is the accommodation reflex for near vision - entirely parasympathetic-controlled.

scaffolds.q: The statement is TRUE - parasympathetic makes the lens bulge for close vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The answer is TRUE. Parasympathetic activation contracts the ciliary muscle, releasing lens tension, allowing it to bulge for near focus.
